1: Stumbling blocks (skandala = traps, snares) were a recognized category of moral harm in Jewish ethics. The term skandalon (from the mechanism of a trap) described anything that caused someone to fall into sin. "Things that cause people to stumble are bound to come" (anendeckton estin tou ta skandala mē elthein) frames the saying with inevitability: the world will always contain occasions of stumbling. But this does not reduce the culpability of those who create them. The "woe" (ouai) signals prophetic condemnation of the deliberate or reckless creation of spiritual hazards for others.
2: The millstone image: a mylon onikos is a donkey-driven millstone -- a large (2+ foot diameter) stone turned by an animal, far heavier than the smaller hand-millstone (mylos cheirikos). Being thrown into the sea with such a stone around one's neck was a recognized form of drowning-execution in the ancient world (Josephus Ant. 14.450 describes rebels drowned in the Lake of Galilee; the Romans used the practice). The comparison is stark: death by drowning would be preferable to the fate of one who causes a child (or a "little one" = a new or vulnerable disciple) to stumble. The severity establishes the principle: spiritual harm to the vulnerable is among the gravest sins.
3: "Watch yourselves" (prosechete heautois) -- the exhortation to community vigilance, not just individual discipline. The pattern of rebuke and forgiveness follows Matthew 18:15-22 (the community discipline passage), though Luke's version is briefer and more focused on the repeated forgiveness (seven times, v4). The Qumran community had elaborate rebuke procedures (1QS Luke 5:24-6:1: rebuke must be immediate, in private, and before witnesses before bringing the matter to the assembly). Jesus' instruction modifies the pattern: the goal is not communal purity but restoration of the brother.
4: "Even if they sin against you seven times in a day and seven times come back to you saying 'I repent,' you must forgive them" -- the "seven times" reflects Jewish conventions about the limits of forgiveness. In Amos 1-2, God warns nations after three transgressions and then again (four); the implied limit suggests that forgiveness has boundaries. Matthew 18:21-22 has Peter asking if seven times is enough, and Jesus responding "seventy-seven times" or "seventy times seven." Luke's "seven times in a day" intensifies the demand: not seven times over a lifetime but seven times in a single day, each time with expressed repentance.
5: The apostles' request for increased faith ("Increase our faith!") is a response to the demand of Luke 17:3-4 -- they recognize that forgiving a brother seven times in a day exceeds normal human capacity and requires a faith larger than they currently possess. The request prostithei hēmin pistin (increase/add to us faith) uses prostithemi, the same word used in Acts for adding converts to the community. Faith here is understood not as intellectual belief but as the enabling resource for radical obedience.
6: The mustard-seed response to the faith-request is paradoxical: even faith as small as a mustard seed (the proverbially smallest seed, cf. Luke 13:19; Mark 4:31) has extraordinary power. "This mulberry tree" (tē sykamiō = the sycamine/mulberry tree, probably the black mulberry, Morus nigra, with very deep roots -- distinguished from the sycamore fig of Luke 19:4) being uprooted and planted in the sea is a deliberately impossible image. The point is not about miraculous tree-transplantation but about the disproportion between the size of faith and the magnitude of its effects: even the smallest genuine faith exceeds the seemingly impossible demand.
7: The parable of the unworthy servant (Luke 17:7-10) is unique to Luke. The social background: servants (douloi = slaves) in the first-century Mediterranean world had no rights and no claim on their master's gratitude. After ploughing all day or tending sheep, the slave's obligation to serve the evening meal continued -- there was no "thank you for your work" moment when the slave could expect special treatment. This was the legal and social reality: the master's acknowledgment of the slave's work was entirely discretionary, not obligatory.
8: The master's instruction to prepare his dinner while still in work clothes before eating and drinking himself is a description of the normal slave economy, not cruelty. The master's table service came before the slave's rest -- this was taken for granted in antiquity. The parable is not endorsing slavery as a moral good but using the understood social structure to make a point about discipleship. The "meta tauta" (after these things) indicates the sequence: master first, servant second. The sequence is simply assumed; no elaboration is needed.
9: "Will he thank the servant because he did what he was told to do?" -- the rhetorical question expects the answer "no." Gratitude (charin = grace, favor, thanks) was owed by a slave to a master who treated him well, not the other way around. The master was not in the slave's debt for the slave performing his assigned duties. The social logic is inverted from modern expectations, where effort deserves reward. In the slave economy, the default was obligation without gratitude from the master.
10: "So you also, when you have done everything you were told to do, should say, 'We are unworthy servants; we have only done our duty'" -- the application to disciples is pointed. The Greek achreios (unworthy, useless) is strong: not merely "humble" but "of no special value to the master." The disciples' obedience (even the extraordinarily demanding obedience of repeated forgiveness) gives them no claim on God's gratitude or reward -- it is simply what is expected of those who have been given the Kingdom. This cuts against any theology of merit: even complete obedience does not create a divine debt.
11: "On his way to Jerusalem, Jesus traveled along the border between Samaria and Galilee" -- Luke's travel narrative (Luke 9:51-19:27) has Jesus moving toward Jerusalem since Luke 9:51. The Samaria-Galilee border region was the mixed territory where Jewish-Samaritan tensions were highest (cf. the Samaritan rejection of Luke 9:52-53). The healing of the ten lepers occurs precisely in this liminal social space, setting up the irony that the returning grateful leper is a Samaritan. The narrative geography underscores the universal reach of Jesus' healing and the unexpected location of gratitude.
12: Ten lepers who stood at a distance (apō = from a distance) -- Leviticus 13:45-46 required lepers to keep a distance from the unaffected population and to call out "Unclean! Unclean!" when anyone approached. The enforced distance of the lepers reflects not just physical health concerns but the social and ritual exclusion that leprosy imposed: they were a group at the margins, having formed their own band of exclusion across the normal Jew-Samaritan social divide (they too could not maintain ethnic distinctions in their shared misery).
13: "Jesus, Master, have pity on us!" -- Epistata (Master) is Luke's characteristic Christological title (Luke 4:38; Luke 5:5; Luke 8:24, 45; Luke 9:33, 49). The plural call reflects both the desperation of corporate affliction and the solidarity of the excluded group. "Have pity on us" (eleēson hēmas) is the language of Psalm prayer (Psalms 4:1; Luke 6:2; Luke 31:9; Luke 86:3) and the Kyrie eleison of the liturgical tradition. Their cry from a distance does not yet involve approach; they call out hoping Jesus will act toward them, not requiring them to violate the distance-protocol first.
14: "Go, show yourselves to the priests" -- the instruction follows Leviticus 14:2-32 exactly: the cleansed leper must present himself to the priest for examination and the eight-day purification ritual before being declared clean. Jesus sends them before the cleansing has occurred -- the healing happens "as they went" (en tō hypeigein autous = while they were going). The faith required is to set out for the priest before the evidence of healing. The contrast with the unhealed Naaman who almost refused Elisha's instruction (2 Kings 5:11-13) is instructive: these lepers obey immediately.
15: One of them, when he saw he was healed, turned back -- the word hypestrepsas (turned back, returned) is significant: all ten were healed "as they went" toward the priests, but only one turned back. The nine were not ungrateful per se; they were following the instruction Jesus had given. The one who returned made a different choice: he prioritized the source of the healing over the next prescribed step. His return is an act of worship priority over religious compliance.
16: He "threw himself at Jesus' feet and thanked him -- and he was a Samaritan." The prostration at Jesus' feet (pesōn epi prosōpon para tous podas autou = falling on his face at his feet) is the posture of worship, not merely social deference. The shock of the narrative: the one who most honored Jesus with gratitude was from the most despised group -- the Samaritan. Luke's consistent pattern: the Samaritan hero (Luke 10:33), the Samaritan leper (Luke 17:16), and the Samaritan evangelization of Acts 8:5-25 together constitute a running Lukan argument for the universality of grace.
17: "Were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine?" -- the question (Jesus addresses the crowd, not the Samaritan) is rhetorical: ten were healed, one returned. The proportion of gratitude (1/10 = 10%) among the blessed contrasts with the completeness of the blessing (100% healed). Jesus' question invites the listeners to notice the disproportion. The nine presumably continued to the priests as instructed -- they were observing Torah. But the Samaritan's return demonstrates that something more than Torah-compliance had occurred in his healing: a relationship.
18: "Has no one returned to give praise to God except this foreigner?" -- allogenes (foreigner, of another race) is the technical term used in the temple for those excluded from the inner courts: the inscription on the temple barrier read "Let no foreigner enter within the barrier and wall around the holy place. Whoever is caught doing so will himself be responsible for his ensuing death" (Josephus Ant. 15.417). The foreigner who cannot enter the temple gives God the praise that nine Jewish men who can enter have not given. The irony is complete.
19: "Rise and go; your faith has made you well" -- the formula of healing-through-faith (hē pistis sou sesōken se = your faith has saved you) appears at the hemorrhaging woman (Luke 8:48), the blind man (Luke 18:42), and here. The language uses sōzō (save/heal) -- the same word as soteria (salvation). The leper's healing already occurred in Luke 17:14; what Jesus says to him alone adds a further dimension: the nine were healed (iathrēsan = were healed, medical term) but the Samaritan was "saved" (sesōken = saved, with its full salvific weight). The returning gratitude is itself a component of what salvation means.
20: "The kingdom of God does not come with your careful observation" -- the Pharisees' question about when the kingdom of God would come reflects the intense eschatological expectation of first-century Judaism: the Zealots expected military liberation, the Essenes expected a divine intervention at a calculated time, the Pharisees debated the conditions for God's act (b. Sanhedrin 97b-98a: "The Son of David will come only when the whole world has become either entirely righteous or entirely wicked"). Jesus refuses the observable-sign framework: parateresis (careful observation/watching for signs) is not the mode of the kingdom's arrival.
21: "Nor will people say, 'Here it is,' or 'There it is,' because the kingdom of God is in your midst" -- the phrase entos hymōn (within you / among you / in your midst) is the most debated expression in Luke. Three interpretations: (1) "within you" = interior/spiritual -- the kingdom is an inner reality (Origen, many mystics); (2) "among you" = present in Jesus himself who stands among them -- the kingdom is present in his person (most modern scholars); (3) "in your grasp/within your reach" = the kingdom is accessible to those who respond. The context (Pharisees asking, Jesus saying they will not see it by external observation) favors interpretation (2): the kingdom is already present in Jesus' ministry before their eyes, but they are not recognizing it.
22: "The time is coming when you will long to see one of the days of the Son of Man, but you will not see it" -- Jesus shifts from the Pharisees to the disciples. The "days of the Son of Man" is apocalyptic language for the era of the Son of Man's visible reign. The disciples' future longing reflects the post-ascension period: between resurrection and parousia, the community will deeply desire the visible return of Jesus. This longing is not a failure of faith but a mark of genuine love for the absent Lord (cf. Revelation 22:20: "Come, Lord Jesus").
23: "People will tell you, 'There he is!' or 'Here he is!'" -- false messianic and apocalyptic claims were common in first-century Palestine: Josephus describes several messianic pretenders (the Egyptian prophet, Theudas, the unnamed prophet from outside Jerusalem: Acts 21:38; Ant. 20.97-98, 169-172; War 2.258-265). After the destruction of Jerusalem (70 CE), the messianic speculation intensified. Jesus warns against responding to such claims: "Do not go running off after them." The disciples must not mistake historical catastrophes for the parousia.
24: "For the Son of Man in his day will be like the lightning, which flashes and lights up the sky from one end to the other" -- the comparison of the parousia to lightning clarifies the previous warning about false sightings: lightning does not need to be pointed out; it is unmistakably self-announcing. When the Son of Man actually appears, there will be no question about location or identification -- he will be visible everywhere simultaneously. This is the antidote to "he is here" or "he is there" claims: the real event will be universally self-evident.
25: "But first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation" -- the "must" (dei) is Luke's divine-necessity marker (Luke 2:49; Luke 4:43; Luke 9:22; Luke 13:33; Luke 22:37; Luke 24:7, 26). The passion prediction is inserted into the apocalyptic discourse to insist that the way to the Son of Man's glorious arrival runs through rejection and death. The disciples' expectation of messianic triumph must be re-routed through the cross. "This generation" (tēs geneas tautēs) carries the prophetic weight of covenantal failure: the generation that rejected the prophets and will reject the Son.
26: "Just as it was in the days of Noah, so also will it be in the days of the Son of Man" -- the Noah analogy appears in Matthew 24:37-39 and here. Jewish apocalyptic used Noah as the type of the faithful survivor amid universal judgment: 1 Enoch 106-107; Jub. Luke 5:1-11; the Dead Sea Scrolls Noah fragments (4Q534-536). The key element is not immorality but normalcy: they were "eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage" -- ordinary life proceeding without awareness of impending judgment. The point is not that ordinary life is sinful but that absorption in it can make people oblivious to eschatological urgency.
27: The Flood narrative (Gen 6-9) provides the historical backdrop: "the flood came and destroyed them all." Noah (Genesis 6:9: "a righteous man, blameless among the people of his time") was saved; the unprepared world perished. The parallel function is preparedness: Noah prepared an ark in obedience to divine warning; the disciples are warned in advance so that they will not be caught unprepared. The flood destroyed all (pantes) -- the universality of judgment contrasts with the particularity of salvation.
28: The Lot analogy is uniquely Lukan among the Synoptics. Lot and the destruction of Sodom (Gen 19) provides the second type. "People were eating and drinking, buying and selling, planting and building" -- the same normalcy as in Noah's day. Sodom's destruction came as a sudden divine intervention after Lot's departure: no observable signs, just the abrupt end of ordinary time. Jewish tradition remembered Lot ambivalently (2 Peter 2:7: "a righteous man"), but his story was unambiguously a type of divine judgment on a civilization that had rejected the divine standard.
29: "But the day Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from heaven and destroyed them all" -- the fire and sulfur (pyr kai theion) of Genesis 19:24 (LXX: pūr kai theion = fire and sulfur) became a standard image of divine judgment in the OT and NT (Isaiah 30:33; Ezekiel 38:22; Revelation 14:10; Luke 19:20; Luke 20:10; Luke 21:8). The destruction "destroyed them all" (apōlesen pantes) parallels the flood's destruction: both judgments were total, sudden, and without warning for the unprepared.
30: "It will be just like this on the day the Son of Man is revealed" -- apokaluptētai (is revealed/unveiled) connects the parousia to the apocalyptic tradition of the revealing of the hidden divine figure (cf. Daniel 7:13-14; 1 Corinthians 1:7; 1 Peter 1:7, 13; Luke 4:13; 2 Thessalonians 1:7: "when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven"). The Son of Man who is currently hidden (or rejected) will be universally unveiled at his coming. The revelation is not a discovery of something new but the uncovering of what has always been true about Jesus' identity.
31: "On that day no one who is on the housetop, with possessions inside, should go down to get them" -- the urgency instruction echoes the Olivet Discourse (Mark 13:15; Matthew 24:17). The Palestinian house had a flat roof accessible by exterior staircase, where people slept in summer and worked (Acts 10:9: Peter praying on the roof). The instruction is: when the moment comes, do not turn back for material possessions. "Let no one in the field go back for anything" -- field work required going out from the house; the instruction is not to return home. These instructions have a concrete application to the flight from Jerusalem in 70 CE and an eschatological application to the final day.
32: "Remember Lot's wife!" -- the briefest warning in the teaching, referencing Genesis 19:26 ("Lot's wife looked back, and she became a pillar of salt"). The tradition about Lot's wife was widely developed in Jewish sources (Wis Luke 10:7: "a pillar of salt stands as a monument to an unbelieving soul"; Josephus Ant. 1.203 says she turned into a pillar of salt "to this day"). Her backward look is interpreted as attachment to what was being left behind: the possessions, the city, the former life. The warning is against the divided heart that looks forward and back simultaneously.
33: "Whoever tries to keep their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life will preserve it" -- this saying appears in multiple forms and contexts throughout the Synoptics (Mark 8:35; Matthew 10:39; Luke 16:25; Luke 9:24; John 12:25), suggesting it was a fundamental and often-repeated teaching. The paradox of life-through-death and death-through-self-preservation is the core of the cross-bearing call (Luke 9:23). In the eschatological context of the Son of Man's coming, the principle applies to the final decision: clinging to earthly life at the decisive moment means ultimate loss.
34: "I tell you, on that night two people will be in one bed; one will be taken and the other left" -- the nighttime setting (night in the Eastern Mediterranean = sleeping time, when people are most caught unprepared) describes the sudden selective nature of the eschatological separation. The "taken" (paralēmphthēsetai) and "left" (aphethēsetai) have been interpreted variously: taken for judgment (as in Noah's flood where the wicked were swept away) or taken for salvation (as in 1 Thessalonians 4:17: "caught up"). Luke's parallel with the flood suggests the "taken" may be those overtaken by judgment.
35: "Two women will be grinding grain together; one will be taken and the other left" -- grinding grain was women's daily work (Exodus 11:5; Numbers 11:8; Isaiah 47:2; Matthew 24:41). Two women grinding together (at a shared hand mill) represents the most ordinary of domestic scenes -- the contrast with the spectacular eschatological event maximizes the unexpectedness. The separation of those who appear identical in situation and activity underscores that the division is based on inner readiness, not outward circumstance.
36: This verse ("Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left") is present in some manuscripts (following the pattern of Matthew 24:40) but absent from the best Lukan manuscripts (P75, Vaticanus, Sinaiticus). It appears to be a harmonizing addition from Matthew's version. The standard Lukan text proceeds from v35 to v37 without this verse. Its absence in Luke preserves the two contrasting examples (nighttime bed, daytime grain-grinding) that together cover the range of times and activities.
37: "Where, Lord?" -- the disciples' question about location is answered with a natural proverb about carrion birds: "Where there is a dead body, there the vultures will gather." The parallel in Matthew 24:28 has the same saying. The vultures (or eagles -- aetoi = eagles, but the image suggests vultures circling over a carcass) gathering around a carcass is a self-locating event: the birds do not need to be shown where the carcass is; they are drawn to it naturally. Similarly, the Son of Man's coming will be self-evident -- asking "where?" is as unnecessary as asking where the vultures are gathering. The obvious sign locates itself.
